ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for chicken burger with corn cup set meal; closest defensible match is chicken sandwich food product (FOODON_00002383). Corn cup side is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Chicken burger) used as closest defensible match. The corn cup side dish is not represented in FoodEx2 as a combined set meal entry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken burger with corn cup set meal combination.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 280 g, comprising a chicken burger (approximately 200 g including bun, patty, and condiments) and a corn cup side (approximately 80 g), as commonly served in Singapore fast-food and hawker-adjacent outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken burger with corn cup — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Chicken burger with corn cup' (cr_id F08020012). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference for chicken burger (USDA SR Legacy #21116) as HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; value cross-referenced and documented below.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Chicken burger with corn cup' as a combined set meal in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the analogous entry for a white-bread-based hamburger bun (GI ~61–72, University of Sydney GI Database) combined with the moderate-GI contribution of sweet corn (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted composite GI of approximately 66 applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (66 × 54.9) / 100 ≈ 36.2 for full 280 g serving; per-100g basis GL = (66 × 19.61) / 100 ≈ 12.9; per-serving GL reported as 22.5 using 170 g available carbohydrate portion of the set. Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 413 mg per 100 g — exceeds the moderate-concern threshold of 400 mg/100 g with limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this meal as the sodium content of 413 mg/100 g (approximately 1,156 mg per 280 g serving) represents more than half the WHO daily sodium target of 2,000 mg; request reduced sauce and seasoning and choose the corn cup over higher-sodium sides.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~66) and 19.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g with 2.17 g dietary fibre providing partial glucose buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this meal occasionally in controlled portions; the dietary fibre (2.17 g/100 g) and protein (8.83 g/100 g) moderate postprandial glucose response, but the medium GI and total carbohydrate load of approximately 55 g per 280 g serving require careful portion management and pairing with low-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from processed burger components alongside high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this meal sparingly due to the combination of elevated sodium (413 mg/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g), and trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from the processed bun and fried patty, all of which contribute to cardiovascular risk; grilled patty and reduced-sauce options are preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Burger with Corn Cup is a Western-style fast-food set meal popular in Singapore, consisting of a chicken patty (typically breaded and fried or grilled) served in a soft white burger bun with lettuce and sauce, accompanied by a side cup of sweet corn kernels. The burger bun contributes refined carbohydrates and gluten, while the chicken patty provides the primary protein source (8.83 g/100 g). The corn cup adds dietary fibre (contributing to the combined 2.17 g/100 g) and a mildly sweet flavour contrast. The overall texture is soft and savoury with a slightly crispy patty exterior. Nutritionally, the meal provides 194 kcal/100 g with moderate fat (8.85 g/100 g) and a notable sodium load of 413 mg/100 g, making it a moderate-energy but high-sodium option. The meal is commonly consumed as a working lunch or quick-service restaurant meal in Singapore. It is tagged as halal and is suitable for non-vegetarian diets. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertension management, while the moderate GI (~66) and fibre content make it a manageable but portion-sensitive choice for Type 2 Diabetes. The trace trans fat and saturated fat from the processed bun and fried patty are relevant considerations for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis risk. The corn cup is a nutritionally preferable side compared to french fries, contributing fibre and micronutrients with lower fat.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for cr_id F08020012 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest match and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database approved source, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names present for Singapore-context Western dish served in Singapore.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #21116 (Chicken sandwich, plain) as the HPB Foodo record does not separately report saturated fat; this is the closest approved-source equivalent and the adaptation is documented per source policy. 2. Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is a trace estimate based on the processed burger bun component; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source and is flagged as an estimate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile for this combined set meal is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry as of 2026. 4. GI value of 66 is an analogous composite estimate; no direct GI measurement for this exact set meal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The dish is classified as a Singapore-context Western fast-food adaptation; multilingual names provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per mandatory Singaporean dish language rules. 6. Halal dietary tag preserved from input source.
